content.text: In agentic systems, the structured description of every tool an agent can invoke is not just documentation — it is a formal contract. When an agent generates a tool call, it must conform to the schema the tool declares; when a tool implementation evolves, it must do so without breaking the agents that depend on its published interface. Treating these schemas as first-class, version-controlled contracts — validated mechanically on every change — is the foundation of stable, maintainable agentic architectures.

A schema contract for a tool definition is a machine-readable specification — typically expressed as a JSON Schema object or an OpenAPI component — that declares the tool's name, a natural-language description, the structure of its input parameters, which parameters are required, and any type or value constraints. Every major LLM provider that supports tool use (Anthropic, OpenAI, Google) consumes this schema at inference time: the model reads it, decides whether to invoke the tool, and if so, emits a structured tool-call object that must conform to the schema it was given.

The term "contract" here carries the same meaning as in consumer-driven contract testing elsewhere in software engineering: a binding specification that both parties — the producer (tool implementation) and the consumer (the agent) — must respect. The schema is the contract. Any code that changes the schema is changing the contract and must do so deliberately, with versioning and backward-compatibility analysis.

This differs from Pact-style contract tests, which record actual HTTP interactions between a service consumer and a service provider and then verify that the provider still satisfies those recorded interactions. Schema contracts for tool definitions are more static: they describe the expected shape of function arguments, not a full HTTP exchange. They are closer to API type definitions or OpenRPC schemas than to Pact cassettes, though the contractual obligation is the same.

Agentic systems are brittle at the integration boundary between the LLM and the tools it calls. When a tool's input format changes — a parameter is renamed, a type is narrowed, a required field is added — the LLM continues generating tool calls based on whatever schema was embedded in its context or system prompt. If that schema is stale, the generated calls will fail validation, and the agent will either produce an error, silently skip the tool, or generate a hallucinated argument to fill the gap.

These failures are particularly insidious because they do not necessarily raise an exception at the Python level. A tool that receives an unexpected argument shape may simply ignore it, return incorrect results, or produce a vague error message that the agent's error handler cannot interpret. Without schema contract enforcement, integration bugs between agent and tool accumulate silently across deployments.

Schema contracts make this class of failure visible at the earliest possible moment — at authoring time, in CI, before any agent ever touches the new tool version. They also provide the formal basis for backward-compatibility analysis: by comparing the current schema against the previous version according to a well-defined ruleset, it becomes possible to classify every change as safe (additive, optional) or breaking (removal, rename, type narrowing).
Schema violations are caught in CI, not in production agent runs where debugging requires replaying expensive multi-step conversations.
A well-authored JSON Schema with a
is simultaneously the runtime contract and the human-readable API reference.
Models generate better-formed tool calls when the schema is precise. Clear
strings and enumerated allowed values reduce hallucinated arguments.
Versioning rules (additive-only changes in a minor version; breaking changes require a new major version) prevent uncoordinated drift between tool authors and agent code.
In systems where multiple agents share a tool registry, schema contracts guarantee that all consumers agree on the same interface, regardless of which agent calls the tool.
CI jobs can compare the current schema against the previously released version and block the PR automatically if any breaking change is detected.

JSON Schema is expressive enough for most tool definitions, but it has limits. It describes structural constraints — types, required fields, enums, patterns — but not semantic constraints. A schema can enforce that
is a non-empty string but cannot enforce that it is a valid city name. A schema can require that
only with custom vocabulary extensions, which not all validators support.

Schema contracts also do not capture the runtime behavior of a tool: they say nothing about latency, side effects, idempotency, or error response shapes. For full API contract coverage, OpenAPI or AsyncAPI specifications are needed, adding authoring overhead. In practice, most agentic systems start with JSON Schema for parameter validation and add OpenAPI only for tools that are also exposed as REST endpoints.
When the tool implementation and the agent consuming it live in different repositories, keeping schemas in sync requires cross-repo CI checks or a shared schema registry.
Maintaining named versions (v1, v2) of tool schemas adds infrastructure: a registry, deprecation notices, and a migration plan for agents still on the old version.

Even with a precise schema, a model may occasionally generate a tool call that fails validation, particularly for complex nested parameter structures. The schema catches this at runtime, but the agent must handle the error gracefully.
JSON Schema validates structure, not intent. A poorly written
field causes the LLM to misunderstand the tool's purpose, even if its arguments pass structural validation.
When a developer registers a tool with an agentic framework, they provide a schema object alongside the callable. The schema is passed to the LLM in the API call's
array (OpenAI's API). The model reads this schema at inference time and, when it decides to invoke the tool, emits a structured object —
field matching the tool name and an
field whose structure should conform to the schema's
The agent framework then has a choice: it can dispatch the tool call directly (trusting the model), or it can validate the
object against the schema before dispatching. Validation before dispatch is the recommended approach. A JSON Schema validator such as Python's

library takes the tool call input and the declared parameters schema and returns either a pass (no errors) or a list of validation errors. If validation fails, the agent can return the error details back to the model in a
message, giving the model a chance to self-correct.

Schema contracts become especially important when tool schemas evolve. The safe evolution rule is: a change is backward-compatible if every tool call that was valid under the old schema is also valid under the new schema. This means only additive changes are safe in a backward-compatible update: adding a new optional parameter, widening an enum, relaxing a pattern constraint. Removing a required field, renaming a parameter, narrowing a type, or changing a default value are breaking changes that require a new schema version.

The diagram below shows the full flow: the agent generates a tool call against the published schema, the validator checks it before dispatch, and two versioned tool implementations coexist behind the validator. Breaking changes require a new version; additive changes are backward-compatible within the same version.

attrs.alt: Schema contract flow for agentic tool definitions

content.text: The schema contract sits at the centre of the flow and plays two roles simultaneously. First, it is the specification fed to the LLM so the model knows how to form a valid tool call. Second, it is the validation rule applied by the framework before the tool implementation is invoked. The validator acts as a gatekeeper: calls that conform pass through to the tool implementation; calls that do not are returned as structured errors that the agent can relay back to the model. The versioning panel clarifies which changes are safe within a version and which require creating a new version.

Schema contract validation is appropriate for any agentic system where tool definitions may change over time or where multiple agents or teams interact with the same tool registry. It is especially important when tools are shared across agent boundaries — for example, when a customer-facing agent and an internal analytics agent both call the same data-retrieval tool.
tool definitions are co-developed by more than one team, or when tools are versioned and deployed independently of the agents that use them.
the agent runs in production and tool call failures have a user-visible cost (wrong answer, failed task, billing event).
for purely prototype-stage tools where the interface is changing rapidly and the overhead of maintaining versioned schemas exceeds the benefit.
Do not use schema contracts as a substitute
for end-to-end integration tests. Schema validation confirms structural correctness; it does not test whether the tool returns the right data for a given input.
Define every tool's parameters as a JSON Schema object in a standalone
field. Store this schema in a version-controlled file alongside the tool implementation.
Derive the schema automatically from a Pydantic model by calling
— this ensures the schema and the Python type annotations stay in sync.
Add a CI step that runs
on every sample tool call fixture in the
directory, confirming that fixtures remain valid under the current schema.
Add a backward-compatibility CI step that loads the previous schema version from git (
At runtime, validate every tool call input before dispatching. Return a
and the validation message if the call does not conform.
When a breaking change is needed, create a new schema version (
), register it under a new tool name (
) or a versioned route, and deprecate the old version with a planned sunset date.
JSON Schema Structure for Tool Definitions
The canonical JSON Schema for a tool definition used by Anthropic's API follows a specific shape: a top-level
string that the model reads to decide when to invoke the tool, and an
object that is a JSON Schema draft 2020-12 object describing the parameters. OpenAI's API calls this
, but the structure is otherwise identical.
Key design rules for well-authored tool schemas: the top-level
is read by the model to decide
to invoke the tool, so it should describe the use case, not just the function signature. The per-property
is read by the model to decide
, so it should give concrete guidance, including examples. Setting
is important: it causes the validator to reject tool calls that include unexpected keys, which would otherwise silently propagate to the implementation.
Basic: Validating a Tool Call with jsonschema
Intermediate: Deriving the Schema from a Pydantic Model

Using Pydantic as the source of truth for tool schemas eliminates the risk of the Python type annotations diverging from the JSON Schema. The Pydantic model is the implementation's type signature; the JSON Schema is generated from it automatically. When the model evolves, the schema updates in lockstep.

# pip install pydantic anthropic
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import Literal
import anthropic
import json


class GetWeatherInput(BaseModel):
    """Input schema for the get_weather tool."""
    city: str = Field(
        description="The city name, e.g. 'London' or 'Tokyo'",
        min_length=1,
        max_length=100
    )
    units: Literal["metric", "imperial"] = Field(
        default="metric",
        description="Temperature units. Defaults to 'metric'."
    )


def build_tool_definition(name: str, description: str, model: type[BaseModel]) -> dict:
    """Build an Anthropic-compatible tool definition from a Pydantic model."""
    schema = model.model_json_schema()
    # Remove Pydantic's $defs and title keys not needed by Anthropic
    schema.pop("title", None)
    schema.pop("$defs", None)
    return {
        "name": name,
        "description": description,
        "input_schema": schema
    }


get_weather_tool = build_tool_definition(
    name="get_weather",
    description="Retrieve current weather for a city. Use when the user asks about weather conditions.",
    model=GetWeatherInput
)

print(json.dumps(get_weather_tool, indent=2))
# Outputs a complete Anthropic-compatible tool definition


# Validate a tool call using the Pydantic model directly
def dispatch_weather_call(tool_input: dict) -> dict:
    try:
        validated = GetWeatherInput.model_validate(tool_input)
    except Exception as e:
        return {"is_error": True, "content": str(e)}
    return {"city": validated.city, "temp": 22, "units": validated.units}


# Use the tool definition in an Anthropic API call
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-5",
    max_tokens=256,
    tools=[get_weather_tool],
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What's the weather like in Paris right now?"}]
)

if response.stop_reason == "tool_use":
    tool_use_block = next(b for b in response.content if b.type == "tool_use")
    result = dispatch_weather_call(tool_use_block.input)
    print(result)

Tool schema versioning follows the same discipline as REST API versioning, adapted for the agentic context. The core question for any proposed change is: does this change break any tool call that was valid before? If yes, the change is breaking and requires a new schema version. If no, the change is additive and safe within the current version.

The most common backward-compatible changes are: adding a new optional parameter (existing calls still work because the new parameter is optional), widening an enum (existing enum values remain valid), relaxing a string constraint such as reducing
, and adding richer descriptions or examples. None of these changes break existing tool calls.
Breaking changes include: removing any parameter (required or optional) that the agent may have been passing, renaming a parameter, narrowing a type (changing
removes previously valid values), adding a new required parameter, and changing
when agents were previously passing extra keys. Each of these requires either a new named version or a coordinated deployment where all agents are updated first.

# scripts/check_schema_compat.py
# Run in CI: python scripts/check_schema_compat.py tools/get_weather/schema.json
# Requires the previous version to be fetchable from git

import json
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path


def load_previous_schema(schema_path: str) -> dict | None:
    """Load the previous version of the schema from the last git commit."""
    result = subprocess.run(
        ["git", "show", f"HEAD:{schema_path}"],
        capture_output=True, text=True
    )
    if result.returncode != 0:
        return None  # File is new, no previous version to compare
    return json.loads(result.stdout)


def check_backward_compat(old: dict, new: dict) -> list[str]:
    """Return a list of breaking change descriptions, empty if none."""
    breaking = []

    old_props = old.get("properties", {})
    new_props = new.get("properties", {})
    old_required = set(old.get("required", []))
    new_required = set(new.get("required", []))

    # Check for removed parameters
    for param in old_props:
        if param not in new_props:
            breaking.append(f"BREAKING: parameter '{param}' was removed")

    # Check for newly required parameters
    for param in new_required - old_required:
        breaking.append(f"BREAKING: parameter '{param}' is now required (was optional or absent)")

    # Check for type changes on existing parameters
    for param in old_props:
        if param in new_props:
            old_type = old_props[param].get("type")
            new_type = new_props[param].get("type")
            if old_type != new_type:
                breaking.append(
                    f"BREAKING: parameter '{param}' type changed "
                    f"from '{old_type}' to '{new_type}'"
                )
            # Check enum narrowing
            old_enum = set(old_props[param].get("enum", []))
            new_enum = set(new_props[param].get("enum", []))
            if old_enum and new_enum and not old_enum.issubset(new_enum):
                removed = old_enum - new_enum
                breaking.append(
                    f"BREAKING: parameter '{param}' enum removed values: {removed}"
                )

    # Check additionalProperties tightening
    old_ap = old.get("additionalProperties", True)
    new_ap = new.get("additionalProperties", True)
    if old_ap is not False and new_ap is False:
        breaking.append(
            "BREAKING: additionalProperties set to false (previously allowed extra keys)"
        )

    return breaking


if __name__ == "__main__":
    schema_path = sys.argv[1]
    current = json.loads(Path(schema_path).read_text())
    previous = load_previous_schema(schema_path)

    if previous is None:
        print(f"No previous schema found for {schema_path}; skipping compatibility check.")
        sys.exit(0)

    issues = check_backward_compat(previous, current)
    if issues:
        print("Schema backward-compatibility check FAILED:")
        for issue in issues:
            print(f"  {issue}")
        sys.exit(1)
    else:
        print(f"Schema backward-compatibility check passed for {schema_path}")
        sys.exit(0)
Consider a production travel-planning agent that uses a
tool. Version 1 of the schema accepts an
(a required ISO-8601 date string). After six months, the product team wants to add multi-city itineraries. The naive approach — changing
from a string to an array — is a breaking change. Instead, the team ships v2 as
(v1) alive for all existing agents. A deprecation note in the tool description informs the model that v2 is preferred.

This pattern — keep the old version stable under its original name, introduce the new version under a new name, then retire the old once all agents have migrated — is the safest migration path. The agent selects between versions based on the descriptions in the tool definitions, and the model will naturally prefer the newer version once the system prompt is updated to encourage it. Monitoring which tool version agents actually call (via structured logging of tool call names) tells the team when v1 usage has dropped to zero and the old version can safely be removed.

Many agentic frameworks pass tool call inputs directly to the Python function without validating them against the schema first. This is a category error: the schema exists at the framework level (in the API call), not at the function level, so Python's own type checking does not enforce it. A model that generates

(an integer instead of a string) will not be caught by Python's type annotations unless the function explicitly validates. Always validate the raw tool call input against the schema before unpacking it into function arguments.
Treating the Schema as Static Documentation

A schema that is written once and never updated accumulates drift as the implementation evolves. Engineers add parameters to the Python function but forget to update the JSON Schema, or they add validation logic to the Python function but not to the schema. The result is that the model generates calls that pass JSON Schema validation but fail at the Python level. Using Pydantic as the single source of truth — with the JSON Schema derived programmatically on every build — eliminates this class of bug.
Breaking Changes Without a New Version

in a tool schema is a breaking change that will cause all agents running against a cached version of the system prompt to generate calls with the old parameter name that now fail validation. If old and new versions of the agent run in parallel during a rolling deployment, some agent instances will succeed and others will fail in a hard-to-debug pattern. Always treat parameter renames and removals as breaking changes requiring a new version, never as safe refactors.
tells the model almost nothing. Thin descriptions cause the model to guess at the expected format, leading to calls with values like
when the tool expects an IATA code or a normalized city name. Invest in the
field: it is the primary channel through which the schema communicates intent to the model, and quality descriptions reduce tool-call errors more than any structural constraint.
Use Pydantic as the single source of truth.
Define tool input types as Pydantic
subclasses, generate the JSON Schema from them with
, and derive the tool definition automatically. This ensures the schema and the Python implementation stay synchronized.
This prevents extra keys in tool call inputs from silently passing through to the implementation. It also makes schema validation errors immediately visible and acts as a security boundary.
For every tool, compare the current schema to the version in the previous git commit. Block merges that introduce breaking changes without a new version designation.
Never pass raw tool call input directly to the tool function. Always run a JSON Schema or Pydantic validation step first and return a structured error to the agent if validation fails.

Store the schema file in the same directory as the tool's implementation code and version them together. A schema change not accompanied by a corresponding implementation change is a red flag in code review.
When a tool version is deprecated, prepend
to the tool's description field. The model reads this when deciding which tool to invoke and will naturally prefer the non-deprecated alternative.
Keep a directory of representative tool call inputs as JSON fixtures. Run
against each fixture on every CI build to confirm that fixtures remain valid under the current schema.

or Pydantic is extremely fast — typically under one millisecond per validation for the schema sizes used in tool definitions. The dominant cost in the agentic tool dispatch path is the tool implementation itself (network calls, database queries), not the schema validation step. Running validation on every tool call in production adds negligible overhead.
For high-throughput systems that dispatch tens of thousands of tool calls per hour, the schema validation step can be further accelerated by pre-compiling the schema once at startup using

and reusing the compiled validator across calls, rather than re-parsing the schema on every invocation. Pydantic v2, which uses a Rust-compiled core, is even faster and is the preferred option for latency-sensitive production systems.
once at module load time. Avoid re-parsing the schema dict on each tool call dispatch.
Pydantic v2's Rust core validates faster than pure-Python jsonschema for complex nested schemas. Prefer it in high-throughput tool dispatch paths.
cross-references) increase the token count sent to the model and slow inference. Keep tool schemas as flat and concise as possible while remaining expressive.
In systems that dispatch multiple tools in parallel (fan-out patterns), schema validation is synchronous per call and adds no concurrency overhead. Each validation is independent.
For tools exposed as REST endpoints
OpenAPI diff and breaking change detection
OpenAPI / AsyncAPI linting with custom rules
Custom rulesets for agentic tool style
Yes — same schema is sent to LLM
Full REST API: paths, methods, params, responses
Partially — input schema can be reused

JSON Schema contracts occupy a unique niche: they are the only contract mechanism that serves both the LLM (by describing the tool in the API call) and the validation layer (by checking the LLM's output before dispatch). Pact is more powerful for full HTTP interaction verification but adds infrastructure (a Pact broker) and is not designed for LLM tool call semantics. OpenAPI spec testing is useful when tools are also REST endpoints and provides richer response-shape validation. Manual integration tests are irreplaceable for end-to-end verification but are too slow and expensive to run on every PR. Python type annotations alone offer no schema for the LLM and no runtime validation of the raw tool call input.

Schema validation before tool dispatch is a security control, not just a quality control. Agentic systems that skip validation create an injection surface: a malicious or corrupted context could cause the model to generate a tool call that passes unexpected arguments to a tool implementation with privileged access (file system, database, external API). A schema with
and strict type constraints limits the blast radius of such injections to the parameters the schema explicitly allows.

Reliability depends on the gap between the schema served to the model and the schema used for validation. If these are different objects — for example, the system prompt was cached with an old schema version while the validator is running the new version — tool calls will fail validation at a rate proportional to the proportion of old-schema calls in active sessions. This is a deployment race condition. Mitigate it by versioning the schema in the tool name (
) and keeping both versions registered until all active agent sessions have expired.
is a security boundary as well as a quality control. Enforce it consistently across all tool schemas in production.

Even state-of-the-art models occasionally hallucinate parameter values that fail validation. The agent's error handler must be prepared to receive validation errors and either retry with the error details or escalate gracefully.
Audit schema changes in code review.
Schema changes have a disproportionate impact on agent behavior. Treat them with the same scrutiny as API contract changes in microservice architectures: require explicit sign-off before merging.

Track the rate of tool call validation failures in production. A spike indicates either a model generating out-of-spec calls (possibly due to a prompt change) or a schema that does not accurately reflect the implementation.
The tool schema is the contract between the agent (consumer) and the tool implementation (provider); both parties must respect it, and changes must be versioned explicitly.
as the single source of truth: derive the JSON Schema from it with
so the Python type annotations and the schema stay synchronized automatically.
Always validate tool call inputs against the schema before dispatching to the implementation; return a structured error to the agent when validation fails so it can self-correct.
Backward-compatible changes (add optional parameter, widen enum) are safe within a version; breaking changes (remove parameter, rename, narrow type) require a new named version such as
Add a CI backward-compatibility check that loads the previous schema from git and classifies every change, blocking merges that introduce unversioned breaking changes.
on every tool schema to prevent unknown keys from passing through to the implementation, providing both a correctness guarantee and a security boundary.

Schema contracts for tools differ from Pact contracts (which record full HTTP interactions) and from OpenAPI specs (which describe full REST endpoints); they serve a unique dual role of guiding the LLM and validating its output.
